straight_beat.name: Archie Ouellette
straight_beat.column:
The loss to Minnesota was a reminder that even in a season where the Blue Jays have moments of brilliance, consistency remains elusive. The Twins didn’t just beat us—they did so with a precision that made it feel inevitable. By the time the final out was made, the scoreboard read 8-2, and the weight of that deficit settled over the ballpark like a fog. For a team that’s supposed to be competitive, this felt like a failure to execute at both ends of the field.  

The pitching staff, which entered the game with a league-average ERA, looked like a collection of question marks. J.A. Happ, who’s been a steady presence in the rotation, allowed five runs in five innings. His stuff didn’t miss, but his command did, leading to walks and easy outs. The real issue, though, was the bullpen. The closer, who’s been a fixture in high-leverage situations, yielded two runs in the seventh, and the setup man followed with a walk. The numbers have it: the Jays’ bullpen has allowed more runs in the seventh inning this season than any team in the league. It’s not just a fluke; it’s a pattern that’s been building since the spring.  

On the offensive side, the Jays’ struggles are less about talent and more about execution. They’ve had the pieces to score runs—Jose Abreu, Vladimir Guerrero Jr., and the emerging sluggers in the middle of the order—but too often, they’ve failed to capitalize. Against Minnesota’s rotation, which has been solid but not

optimist.name: Margot Tremblay
optimist.column:
The loss to Minnesota stings, sure. Eight runs? That’s a whole lot of pain for a team that’s been trying to claw its way back into the playoff race. But let’s not forget: this is the same team that once made the Dome shake like a tambourine in ’93. That’s not a metaphor, pals. That’s a memory. And memories, as we’ve learned, are just the first chapter of a story.  

The Blue Jays are 6-9, yes, and 2.5 games behind in the AL East. But here’s the thing: this isn’t the first time they’ve been in this position. It’s not even the second. In fact, if you count the

pessimist.name: Doug Purvis
pessimist.column:
The Blue Jays lost again, and for the sixth time in nine games, and now they’re 6-9, 2.5 games behind in the AL East. It’s the same old story, eh? The same old excuses. Rogers sold us out for a cable bill and a photo op. Every year it’s the same sermon — and the pews are getting thin.  

You think the Twins are the problem? No, not really. The Twins are a good team, sure, but the Blue Jays aren’t even close to being competitive. They’re not just losing games; they’re losing
